chunk_summary: This section discusses safe practices for parenteral nutrition (PN), focusing on venous access choice and complete PN order content, including guidelines for nutrient dosing, monitoring, and infection prevention.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for choosing central versus peripheral venous access for PN.
Learn how to write a complete and unambiguous PN prescription.
Recognize age-based protein dosing targets and adjustments for clinical conditions.
Identify key safety measures for PN administration, including monitoring and line management.
key_facts: Central venous access (CVC or PICC) is preferred for full PN due to high osmolarity and glucose concentrations.
Peripheral PN is suitable only short-term with lower dextrose concentrations and close monitoring for thrombophlebitis.
Age-based protein dosing: ~2.5–3.5 g/kg/day for preterm infants, ~3 g/kg/day for term infants, and ~1–2 g/kg/day for older children.
PN prescriptions must include total fluid volume, infusion duration, amino acids, dextrose concentration, lipids, electrolytes, multivitamins, and trace elements.
Baseline labs (e.g., electrolytes, glucose, triglycerides, liver enzymes) should be obtained before starting PN, with stepwise advancement and monitoring.
reference_quote: Central venous access (CVC or PICC) is preferred for full PN because osmolarity and glucose concentrations are usually too high for peripheral veins.
